chunks.text: Yes... quite a bit of time. Yes. Good chunk of time. How are you, Christine? We've got everyone's raring to go. Look, I'll invite you both to make some opening comments if you wish, introduce your team, and welcome to all the officials. Yeah, sure. Well, firstly, thanks for having me. Looking forward to a good conversation. There's lots of stuff I could talk about.

Obviously, housing and urban development spans lots of issues. Maybe just a brief update on Going for Housing Growth, which is the centerpiece of the government's policy agenda here. Three pillars: land supply, infrastructure, funding and financing, and incentives.

They all work together obviously, but it's an attempt that builds on, frankly, the last 10 years or so of urban development and housing growth work. Really kickstarted in 2016 through the Unitary Plan process following the Auckland reforms of 2010 through '12, and the Auckland Unitary Plan, which is now internationally studied as and cited as an example of up-zoning help and affordability.

And then obviously the NPSUD, National Policy Statement on Urban Development of the last government. So it's in a lineage of reforms, which are starting to make a difference in the housing market. So land supply, for which resource management reform is very important, alongside, to some extent, fast track.

The budget provides quite a substantial sum of money, 294 million, to roll out resource management reform, the digital back end, and the standards as part of that. That's not actually within Vote Housing and Urban Development, but is important and there's a range of work underway at the moment around national direction in the new planning system, which will be almost like the NPSUD Mark 2, to do things like set housing growth targets.

We're just working out exactly what that looks like in practice. More mixed use, up-zoning around transit corridors, of which we've done some things already obviously through legislation, and the NPSUD already exists. So that work is very important. Pillar two is the funding and financing work.

There's a bill that's just come back into the house, Infrastructure Funding and Financing Amendment Bill, which makes some amendments to the Infrastructure Funding and Financing Act, to allow off balance sheet borrowing, for essentially private finance to get housing away. Has traditionally been regarded as a kind of greenfields approach, which is useful and important and deals with some of the debt constraints that face councils and their balance sheets.

But the new act, once the bill's passed, will allow IFF levies to be used for things other than just greenfields housing, so for example, transport projects, both highways and public transport projects. So it's an advance in that sense. And we're also got quite a lot of detailed policy work underway around development levies. So a flexible financing system for a flexible planning system. There's a lot of detail around that.

We're hoping to get a bill into the house in the next few months, before the election. Around that, there's been two rounds of consultation around the development levy work. So that's pillar two. And then pillar three, is the incentives which we have made progress on in this year's budget. Obviously, there's $400 million set aside for these.

The Incentive for Growth Fund, which gives effect to the Act New Zealand coalition agreement obviously, and actually National Party manifesto. Sometimes people forget we campaigned on it too. Which is designed to change the political economy of housing so that councils see housing growth as not just a burden, which they often see it as, but they actually gain a financial benefit out of that.

And so there's 400 million set aside, 100 million per year. And we're in the process of setting up a scheme, which will, first amount of money will be paid out from the 1st of April 2027, to... And it's essentially a form of progressive incentives, so the harder you go and the more houses you get through, the better you do as a council. So get the incentives in the right place.

So the more you do, the more money there is. And we spent quite a lot of time designing that, and pretty proud about that. There's heaps of other stuff in the budget, but I'm conscious you've got... I've just noticed- Maramena... Maramena online as well. Kia ora, Maramena. And so there's heaps of other stuff we could talk about, but maybe that's just a good place to start.

Brilliant. Thank you. First question, Arana. Kia ora. Thanks for coming in. Your budget raises rents for social housing tenants. The Housing First providers are funded by you for 2,735 places, but as of March, they are housing 3,630 people because of desperate need, and more than three-quarters of the additional social homes that you've announced haven't been built. Why should anyone look at the announcement around housing you made today as anything other than cynical?

What announcement are you talking about? The homelessness announcement- Oh, right... which is too little too late. Oh, right. Well, I will hand over to Minister Pōtaka who has responsibility for this. But you've raised a series of assertions there. It's quite interesting.

Just at a high level, let's be honest, homelessness is not a new problem in New Zealand. It got worse between 2018 and 2023 despite billions being spent. So if it was a money problem, we'd have solved it. There's a range of different issues going on, and the underlying problem of course is the planning system I've just talked about.

And that's why fixing the fundamentals of our housing market are critical. And doing that will do the most to solve the underlying problems with housing unaffordability in this country. So that's why we're so focused on it. But it is also true that there will always be social disadvantage and deprivation, and people who fall through the cracks of social support for whom the government has an obvious and legitimate role to support, and that's why we fund a range of providers in our cities

to help those people. And so we did that package in October last year, obviously, which has proven really successful. Tama's got the numbers, and get him to take them through them. And then, because it's been successful, particularly the outreach services, we've just put some more money into a range of those outreach services and, in fact, expanded the range of areas that are able to access some of that money, which will be well received by those regions.

So I kind of reject your characterization of that, but you're obviously entitled to your views around that. I don't know, Minister, do you want to? Yes, thank you for your question. No doubt, there are some serious challenges for many people who are suffering serious housing deprivation out there. And, of course, the Tier 1 statistic that we often refer to, and actually other reporters refer to as well, is the Census 2023 stats. And in some, at least one area of the four areas, that four spheres of that assessment, that being emergency housing, there's been

a significant reduction in the number of people, particularly children, living in emergency housing from around 3,342 when we came into office to around 500, just under 500 over the last few weeks. And, of course, a significant reduction in the number of tamariki and children living in emergency housing by over 3,000. We think that is progress. For the most part, we know where people are going to after they leave emergency housing.

But there are a number of people who didn't even make it to emergency housing. They've been on the street, living rough, living without shelter. And there are a number of initiatives and matters that we have undertaken in order to make sure that we'll reach those people through often community housing providers and others.

Nearly half a billion dollars is spent on transitional rapid rehousing and other forms of assistance for those in severe housing deprivation. And we want to make sure that that works efficiently. But we also recognize sometimes we need to take more steps in order to provide the appropriate pathways to support people, a la what Minister Bishop mentioned, the September, October announcements last year, where we added 300 Housing First places funded in Auckland, Hamilton, Wellington, and Christchurch.

And the great thing is that as a result of that funding, we were also able to communicate expectations and drive accountability in existing funded places that had not been filled. So since we made that announcement, about 650 or so individuals have come out of the Housing First register and actually moved into those houses, and sometimes they've moved on to transitional and other accommodation. But that, in my view, is very considerable progress. And as a result, what we've seen is stabilizing, and in some instances, in some cities, a reduction of those people who are living

rough. A great example, of course, being Auckland, where a number of community housing providers, and in fact some of the reports are that it's reduced considerably. And that's good news. We also have a range of initiatives around building homes. And you'll know Lisa Woolley out at VisionWest and the likes of some of the Maori housing providers, Willie Te Aho, well-known assertive personality from the East Coast-... building a number of homes out at Turanga Tangata Rite adjacent to the Gisborne Hospital.

Tuku Morgan, Waikato, and a range of other initiatives led by Maori housing providers. And then, of course, the 1,500 homes funded through Budget 24 throughout the country, which has five strategic partners, one of whom, Ngāi Te Tuoro Anga Tiro, opened another 10 homes yesterday in Porirua, which I attended.

I can give you an update on the- Yeah... numbers on that, because there was an assertion that they haven't been built or they're slow. The Budget 24 funding for 1,500 places, which was funded through canceling the first home grants, so in other words- 446. Yeah, correct. But the funding kicked in on the 1st of July 2025, and it takes time to build houses. So this is the point, is that the funding was allocated in the Budget 24 to start from the 1st of July 2025. The current expected delivery of those 1,500 places is

actually 1,549, so we're getting more bang for buck than we initially estimated. Budget 2025 places, which was estimated at 550 to 600 to be delivered by November 2026. The current expected delivery is 660. So we're making really good progress there.

And for the first time, thanks to Budget 2026 funding through the flexible fund, which starts on the 2028/9 period and goes through into the out years. For the first time, the sector now has a four-year consistent pipeline of social housing places funded that they can plan for.

So what used to happen was there'd be a big dollop of money, and then every couple of years would go by, and there'd be another big dollop of money. That's actually not what the sector wants. What they want is a four-year funded pipeline, which they now have. And in a time of very tight fiscal constraints, I'm really proud of what we've been able to do in providing to the sector as a consistent pipeline delivered flexibly, and we can talk about the flexible fund and how it works. And at the same time, we've come in behind the Community Housing

Funding Agency or Authority, and we've done the CHFA- And we've done the, uh, Loan Bank Guarantee Scheme, and the Reserve Bank's- All right... just recently changed the capital ratios for social housing. All of which is to say, and it gets complicated, but it's important, the debt costs for the community housing providers that are borrowing against essentially the money we give them through the long-term contracts has gone down. Uh, the flexible fund will mean that we, as a government, capture the savings, the interest savings, as a result of that.

Uh, and so we're building a much more sophisticated and commercially viable social housing system that is going to deliver houses where they're now needed most. So let me get this right. Hang on. I would like to- In, in this 10-minute answer, your argument is that homelessness is getting better because of your initiatives. When your own homelessness insights report- No, no. I encourage you to list- Hang on. Hang on... Hang on a minute. You could- If you have 10 minutes, maybe I could ask you a question.

Okay. Well, if the, if this- In the homelessness insights report released today, which points to indicators like in this year, the number of people who've been discharged from hospital to no fixed abode has gone up 30% on your watch. You're, you've come to this committee, and you've told us that what you're doing is working.

Okay. So, uh, Mr. Chair- You're not even listening to the answers. If you're just going to basically sit there and listen to us and then just throw random assertions out to try and get on the news, like- You have another 10 minutes... good luck. I mean, like, that's an approach to being a... doing the estimates. If you want to engage in an actual intellectual conversation around what the housing system works and how it works and how it's going to get better- Maybe I could ask you another one... maybe. Like, well, I'm, I'm up for that.

But, you know, I think you're doing the committee and frankly the public a disservice with this sort of approach. Okay. So, so in relation to the PR release today, um, around transitional housing and outreach. What we've recognized is that, uh, we took action into September. Uh, we deployed some money into four locations, uh, Auckland, Hamilton, Christchurch, and I always say Hamilton twice.

And, uh, we recognize that there are additional locations that also have s- significant, uh, need. So we've, uh, added some further money into Whakatane, Waimakariri, Napier, New Plymouth, Whanganui, and Tauranga. Uh, now that doesn't necessarily cover the entire country, we recognize that, but, uh, we've also got existing arrangements in place in most of those cities that we can bolt onto, and we can activate that very, very quickly.

I think the other thing to be, uh, aware of, two things, uh, in relation to the document that you referred to. They've been very clear in the last year and a half, uh, that, uh, the credibility, uh, of the data that's supplied is variable. It is not tier one data- Fair enough... à la census. And separately, the policy decisions that have been made by this government, uh, cannot be, uh, ultimately, uh, connected to as the mover and shaker of numbers. In fact, there's a whole range of circumstances, like homelessness-

It's your data in this report... like, um, like homeless... No, it's not actually. It's the data that's collated and gathered- It's not, it's not our data... Sorry, I'll give you an example from last- A, it's hard data, and B, it's based on going to talk to councils. Not our data... Yeah. I'll give you an example. Last year, we found that one of the data sources was the number of people that was ringing into council to report on people they've seen on the street.

That was happening for the Rotorua District Council. That data was being used to fund and populate the report. Now, I wouldn't call that tier one data. You, you might. I don't. So I think we have to be clear about the credibility of data and don't buy into, uh, the data as being the be all and end all. We have to go beyond some of that reporting and find out what exactly is going on in each place, because it's not the same in each place.

If you rely on just the data you've got right now, the number of people that were seen as living without shelter in Auckland has gone from 950 to about 540 over the last 12 months. Now, we would like to think, and we do think, that a lot of that is to do with the work that we've done to drive accountability in Housing First providers who had 130 places that are now fu- filled, that were funded but not filled in September. Now, we think that's a good thing.

Did over 207 places in Auckland that have now been populated as a result of Minister Bishop's and my announcement in September. Why do corrections data points show that there has been an increase in the last year of 10% of prisoners who are- Yeah... being released- Oh, we do have the data... sleep under church eaves- So you raise, like, you raise a really good point.

Yeah. That's a good point. You, you raise a really good issue. And, um, there's an Auditor-General, um, report into, uh, exits from prisons, um, into, into the community, and it's, that's an area that's been a significant issue for quite some time. It's actually a bit of a passion area of mine. Uh, and it's an area we're doing quite a lot of work on.

This is where the review of social housing comes in. Um, and, and what I'm calling mark two of the flexible fund. So, um, just to give you a brief background. What we did in the flexible fund in last year's budget is we funded an initial tranche of places into the, the social housing places that could be deployed. In the first iteration of that, uh, we took a place-based approach.

So in other words, we said, "Okay, rather than just kind of pick and mix and throw the money out and see where it lands, let's get granular and particular around where the money goes." Uh, and, um, we've, as a result of that, prioritized a series of places around the country where the need is the greatest, uh, and allocated specifically, uh, Northland, for example, um, uh, Tairāwhiti, East Coast, um- Bay of Plenty... Bay of Plenty, uh, and, and, um- There's Hamilton, Auckland... Hamilton, Auckland. There's some for Auckland and Hamilton.

Yeah. So that was kind of mark one. We can give you the numbers ex- on the exact breakdown of it. I just have it right in front of me. Tama's going to look for it. Mark two of the flexible fund is going to take a much more particular cohort-based approach to what sort of houses we build. My, my mantra is basically, right houses, right place, right people.

You could probably add to that, right support. What I mean by right house is building more one and two-bedroom units. By right place, you look at social deprivation and, and where the housing need is, and there's a range of different metrics to work that out. And then the right people is taking a step back and saying, "Okay." Where do we get-- All housing's important, and housing everyone's important, but there's clearly a group of people for whom housing support and having a house is highly significant to their life chances.

Okay? And they face huge access barriers into getting into housing, warm and dry houses. Now, we're doing a piece of work. So rather than just have our reckons around that, we're doing a piece of work, HUD is doing a piece of work with the Social Investment Agency around that. Because we actually have, inside central government, an enormous amount of data around people and where they go and their trajectory through life.

We haven't historically used that data in a particularly sophisticated way to make sure we're building the right sort of houses. So, for example, my intuition, which I suspect you agree with, is that one priority cohort should be ex-prisoners. Because we know, in fact, we do have good data on this, people who leave prison, they don't go into a house. They're meant to. They often don't. I've met them personally.

They don't go into a house and have the right support around them. What are the chances that they end up back in prison? I would say probably quite high. Mm-hmm. And the most expensive form of social housing the government provides is prison. Mm-hmm. Actually, another extremely expensive form of-... housing the government provides is acute mental health beds. Mm-hmm. And I met-- I visited a provider last week in Auckland, which was actually funded by the last government.

It's a trial project, funded by the last government through the Homelessness Action Plan. It's a three-year trial between the Communities of Refuge Trust social service provider with the social housing places provided through central government, in which people leaving acute mental health crisis clinics are able to be discharged into social housing. And I got told in that meeting, and this really winds me up.

Someone in that acute crisis center lived there for four years because government agencies in the social sector could not organize a bed for him. So that's the public policy problem we're trying to solve, is how does the system let that happen?

Four years. So leave aside the fiscal cost, which is obviously extremely expensive for the health system. How does the system allow that to happen? So we have to do better, and that's what we're trying to solve, is making sure that people leaving mental health units or prison or victims of family violence, for example, have a place to go and get their lives back on track with the support being provided. So that's the driver of what we're trying to do.

And we're not there yet, and we need to do the work and do the analysis and let the evidence drive the policy. But as you can tell, I'm relatively exercised about it because the system is failing too many people at the moment. Anyway. And the last- You were complaining about lack of questions, so fair enough. No, I was complaining that your answers are so long.

We've answered two questions in 22 minutes. Well, you've got an hour and 40. Away you go. Let me ask you a quick one then. Getting granular and specific about it, as you say, there is an increase of 7.4% of high schoolers who are experiencing that sort of housing- Yeah... insecurity. Yeah. How does upping social housing rents on about 150,000 families- Yeah... with high school-aged students- Yeah... make teenagers more secure in their homes?

Yeah. Well, not all of those, not all the people will be the cohort you're talking about. But, what we're doing with the review of social housing is, trying to create a more equitable system. And, we've published all the analysis around this, and it's publicly available.

So it kind of goes back to the points I made before, which is who is social housing for and who are the people that we're trying to prioritize into social housing? And given limited resources and constraints, you do have to prioritize, and that is just the reality. No matter who's in government, that is the reality.

And there's a group of people for whom social housing, they earn enough, despite the fact that they're in social housing, they earn enough to actually not be in a social house. It's 29? 29's the number, right? Yeah. 29%, so not quite a third, but not far off.

A third of people earn enough in terms of income to be able to access a lower quartile rent in the private market. And so there's an inequity in the system at the moment, and there are people who are in the same circumstance in the private market as people in the same circumstances in social housing on an income level.

And one group of people pay 25% of their income for rent, and another group of people pay upwards of 35, 40, even higher percent of their weekly income, even though they're in the same financial circumstances. Now, I think most people would say that's not fair. But that is the system that we've created. Now, there's lots of reasons as to why that system's been created.

We are grappling with that system and trying to fix it, but I accept that it's not easy and it's built up over 20 years. So we're on the start of the journey to try and fix that. What the officials would tell you is horizontal inequity, which is a prosaic way of saying unfairness. I want to ask you a supplementary on this, actually, and just to put it in context.

So I worked as a trial lawyer and mental health advocate for- ... a decade or so from Invercargill to Auckland. And people who are released from prison is one issue. They're basically almost outcasts in terms of the system, so it's hard to get into accommodation. The other one though, in Auckland, for example, I worked in the Auckland Alcohol and Drug Treatment Court for a couple of years, as well among other things.

And homeless people there, they usually, almost always actually, they always had a place to go that had been organized for them. But they always ended up back on the street. Why? Because they had serious mental health issues. Yeah. And serious alcohol and drug addiction issues.

Yeah. So my question is what work is being done to- Yeah... assess that? Because if you have just say a room or a house, that's not going to cut it- Yeah... in terms of making sure those people stay in that accommodation. Yeah. No, so that's the other part of the equation, right?

So it's all very well to focus on the house or the rental, and that's important. The next equally as important part of the equation is the services, the support provided. And I am just intensely frustrated at the disaggregation of the system. Health does its thing, MSD does its thing, HUD does its thing.

HUD funds a bunch of providers, MSD funds a bunch of providers. Health does it. There'd be any other number of other government agencies. Corrections got its own thing going on. This is part and parcel of the Leviathan state, right? And its inability to provide effective services for people most in need.

It's extraordinary how much money we spend as a country and yet still have guys in mental health units for four years, or the guy in New Plymouth who was in a motel for three years in a studio room with his-- a disabled man in a motel room with his son in a studio room in New Plymouth. Even though he'd been allocated a social house because ACC couldn't organize him a ramp, and then they waited nine months for the resource consent from the council for the ramp. In the meantime, he just stayed in the motel room with the government paying. It's not actually about the money; it's more about

the life opportunities denied to him and the social cost of that. The system fails too many vulnerable people. So we've got a piece of work underway, which we haven't made as much progress on as I would've liked, around getting services that are provided by HUD and MSD, and other providers much more joined up and a much more individualized focus.

And we haven't made as much progress on that. But successive governments have tried and failed. We're going to give it a go. I think part of the, I suppose, more cohesive end of town, Joe, Mr Chair, is around the provision of Maori housing throughout the country, where often Maori housing providers will bring these additional gels to enable a cohesive and enduring solution for whanau.

So whether it's Toitū Taiāwhio who are hardwired into the provision of health and other services in Gisborne or places like Whakauae over in Rotorua, where they're building hundreds of houses up by the airport. They have their own community housing provider who's working alongside the tribal corporation who's building the houses, to come together to provide that sort of pastoral or monarchy support and care for people who are moving into the house and come from a very difficult space.

So what we've found is that Kai Mauora really builds the house and makes sure all the lights are on and the mailboxes are sorted. Whereas some of these other providers are much more suited to providing a, I call it a whanau ora experience, but a more enduring experience with that wraparound support. And as a result, that's part of the context for us leaning hard into community housing providers- Mm... and being a bit more agnostic about where we put our boots here in order to get to a solution. That tour is a great example, too.

They've got Ora Tour, they've got the housing provider, they've got employment going on. They've got all these things around them that can provide a bit more of a community and a home rather than just a house. Mm. Makes sense. Supplementary, Helen. Can I ask you, last year we were here talking about Wesley- Which one, sorry?

This was Wesley, the Wesley development. Oh, yeah. 6,000 houses. Nice one. That development, when you were waxing lyrical, as you do, about the nature of affordable housing and the need for supply. That was 6,000 houses. They were going to be close to transport. They were not pushed out into the suburbs. They were right in the heart of the new city, given CRL.

Yeah. Why wouldn't we prioritize that sort of development, which actually just connects up our city and keeps it as one that is diverse and where people understand each other? Yeah. Well, the Mount Roskill large-scale project is currently underway.

Who's here from KO? Sorry, I should've- Tracy Taylor... I should've introduced everybody. Might get Tracy to- Tracy, do you want to come up? Come and talk or you can LSP this one. So my notes say 37 hectares have been developed, enabled capacity for 2,367 homes across the Mount Roskill area. 571 homes are currently under construction.

1,185 homes already completed. They've done some relocations. That's actually a different area, so this is the Wesley development. Yeah, Wesley West stages one and two. Yeah, it's part of the Mount Roskill LSP. We've got a school there that's got 109 kids. Yeah. We've got another one that was rebuilt with a really low roll. Yeah. The whole community is boarded up It needs, actually, the attention.

It was all ready to go. You would've had 6,000 new houses. And but, think you had not- It's not the same development. It's across. Part of it. There's one over there. Part of it. One-- This is Wesley. This is- It's part... Yeah. It's part of the- Well, you can tell me whether what I'm talking about, which I know you'll be well familiar with, is going ahead. I want to know it is.

Yeah. I mean, I think, yeah. Kia ora koutou. I'm Tracy Taylor, Chief Executive of Kainga Ora. So it is part of the portfolio that the minister is talking about. This part, at the moment, is under review, and we've sort of got a business case at the moment that we're running through in terms of that, so it is paused.

But we have done large parts of other pieces of this project. I do just want to remind the committee also that we have a board that we're governed by, and also these decisions need to go through there. And that is something that, there's a few that we've got sort of in train at the moment in terms of discussions and what we do, particularly looking into the next financial year.

Okay. Can I ask another one? Last one. So you talked about the joined-up nature of things. And I take your point. It drives us all crazy. I've got a development which is the Carrington development. Mm-hmm. Yep. There's not enough schooling. If this goes ahead in the way that it's projected to, there is simply not enough schooling in the area.

We have three and a half thousand at Max. We have Avondale full. We have Western Springs full. Yep. We need another secondary school. Yep. We have a property there, which is the Carrington development. Yep. And they've taken the tiles off the top of the roof, and that's letting the water in, and it's eroding a building that could be a stunning school.

Yeah. It could be a community center, it could be a library, it could be a school. Where's the joined up-ness for the community with regard to where is the new secondary school? Because people won't buy the apartments- Yeah... that are right there, and they're beautiful. I've been through them. They're fantastic. But they don't know what's going on in the area.

Rain, Electric NP, let's have another. I saw your video. Turned up on my Insta. Must've been the- Compliments. Follow up. Must've been the algorithm. Got Tamitha came next, so must've known I was turning up to the committee. Anyway, so, we might follow up on that. I actually saw your video about the rain and stuff. We might follow that up. Do you know what's going on there?

Kia ora. Yes. Kia ora. Jessica from HUD. Yes. So that part of Carrington, that development is, we're now in a development agreement with the ropu. So, that building is as is always difficult with buildings that are heritage buildings, where the responsibility is ensure that they don't deteriorate. But the development of that building is 100% under the purview of the developer, which is Matua, who will come in that instance.

Isn't that a problem? Where like we haven't-- Basically, every room just- Oh, sorry. Actually, we could-- Can I-- Hang on a moment, because there's another couple points there. So- You're right... so this is the Land for Housing Development, which has got a long history. It started life as the Crown Land program. It's continued. The Carrington development was actually, it's basically a last government thing. We've carried it forward because it's a good development.

And so it's a complicated financial arrangement, but basically, the ropu there is developed. They've got control of it now, eh? They do. Yeah. So we might have a chat to them about sorting out the- Thank you. I'd love that. But on your wider issue- Yeah... and you should contact them. Join up thing. We will contact them, but you should also do that. I've tried all sorts to those people. Yeah. Okay. On the wider issue around planning and schools and... You are right.

We do this badly in New Zealand. That's what we're trying to solve through RMA reform, spatial plans, precinct plans, integrated planning at a local level. The creation of M-CERT so that we're not thinking about housing, transport, infrastructure, and local facilities all in disconnected ways and discombobulated bubbles inside central government.

We're thinking about it in an integrated way. This is part of what we're trying to do. You are right. And just on the school thing, my numbers tell me 4,500 homes on 40 hectares of land in Mount Albert, your electorate. But it's not 4,500 homes tomorrow, right? It's what? 20 years probably. 10 to 15 years.

10 to 15 years. So there's time to build a school. And should we have got ahead of that and reserved the land in advance and thought about that? Yes, you are right. You're absolutely right. That's why, for example, down at Drury, we've announced that we're thinking about a hospital there, and we've started to make progress on some of these issues. But you are right that that is something New Zealand does badly.

Thank you. Just in response to Helen's question, Mr. Chair. There's a longstanding intention amongst the three development ropu that schooling will be created at that site, and I don't think that that intention has dropped away. Yeah.
